Childhood lead poisoning prevention | Public Health | County of Santa Clara

Childhood lead poisoning prevention
Español
Tiếng Việt
中文
Stay connected: Get email updates
Lead is toxic to everyone, especially children under 6 years of age. Lead poisoning occurs when lead builds up in the body. Even small amounts of lead can make it harder for your child to learn, pay attention, and behave. There are many ways your child could come in contact with lead in your home and community.
Common Sources of Lead
Lead can be found in many places inside and outside your home. Exposure to any of these sources can hurt your child. Here are some common sources that may contain lead.
Lead in old, deteriorated, or chipping paint
Lead in furniture painting/refinishing
Lead in some pottery and utensils (glazed pottery or pottery that is worn, handmade, or from outside the USA)
Lead in some foods and brightly-colored spices from outside the USA
Lead in some imported candy
Lead found in chapulines (fried grasshoppers)
Lead in traditional cosmetics (like kohl, surma, sindoor, or kajal)
Lead in remedies (like azarcon, greta, ayurvedic medicine, or pay-loo-ah)
Lead in children's jewelry
Lead in some toys (especially imported toys or old/vinyl toys)
Lead in fishing sinkers
Lead in plumbing materials (including solder and service lines)
Lead exposure on the job
Lead in contaminated soil
Lead in air pollution (aviation gas, industrial areas, shooting ranges, construction areas)
Health Effects of Lead
Lead poisoning can make it hard for your child to learn and pay attention.
Lead poisoning may cause long-term health problems.
Children may not look or act sick, so, it's important to ask their healthcare provider about a blood lead test.
Lead poisoning is
preventable.
Take action today!
If you think your child might be at risk for lead exposure, ask your child's doctor and have your child
tested for lead
.
CLPPP
works to prevent the harms caused by lead poisoning in children and young adults from birth to age 21.
Our services include:
Case management:
Coordinating care and support for children and youth diagnosed with lead poisoning.
Environmental investigation:
Identifies and analyzes sources of lead poisoning such as paint, dust, and soil.
Healthcare provider collaboration:
Works with healthcare providers to help children receive the appropriate follow-up care.
Community outreach and education:
Increase community awareness about lead poisoning and ways to prevent lead exposure.
Presentations are available
in English and
Spanish
upon request. Contact us at
408-992-4900
to learn more or schedule a session.
Free
educational materials on lead poisoning and prevention in several languages for pickup or delivery.
